heroOverview: Fasciolopsiasis is a foodborne intestinal trematode infection caused by Fasciolopsis buski — the largest intestinal fluke parasitising humans, reaching up to 7.5 cm in length — which attaches to the wall of the small intestine and causes diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and nutritional impairment. An estimated 2-10 million people are infected, predominantly in South and Southeast Asia where aquatic plants grown in snail-contaminated water are consumed raw. Humans and pigs acquire infection by ingesting metacercariae encysted on the surface of freshwater plants such as water caltrop, water chestnut, lotus, and morning glory. Unlike liver or lung flukes, Fasciolopsis buski does not migrate to distant organs; its entire pathological effect is exerted in the small bowel, where large numbers of flukes attached to the mucosa cause mechanical irritation, protein-losing enteropathy, and malabsorption. Praziquantel is curative in a single dose.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 2-10 million people infected, primarily in Asia (WHO and systematic review data)
keyStats.demographics: Endemic in Bangladesh, India, China (especially Guangdong and Zhejiang provinces), Thailand, Vietnam, and the Philippines; children and adults in farming communities who consume raw aquatic plants are most affected
keyStats.avgAge: No strict age peak; children in rural endemic areas often carry the heaviest worm burdens
keyStats.globalCases: Classified as a neglected tropical disease; case burden has declined in some areas following improved sanitation and pig management but persists in subsistence farming communities

definition: Fasciolopsiasis (ICD-10: B66.5) is a small intestinal helminthic infection caused by the trematode Fasciolopsis buski, the largest intestinal fluke of humans. The parasite's life cycle mirrors other food-borne trematodes: eggs passed in human or pig faeces hatch in freshwater, infective cercariae emerge from Segmentina and Hippeutis snail intermediate hosts, and cercariae encyst as metacercariae on aquatic plants. Infection occurs when humans or pigs eat these plants raw. After excystation in the duodenum, juvenile flukes attach directly to the mucosa of the duodenum and jejunum by suckers and mature into adults over 3-4 months. There is no hepatic or pulmonary migration phase. Adult flukes can number in the hundreds in heavy infections and produce large amounts of immunogenic excretory-secretory products that drive a local and systemic inflammatory response. The resulting syndrome ranges from asymptomatic carriage in light infections to severe protein-losing enteropathy, hypoalbuminaemia, generalised oedema, and intestinal obstruction in very heavy infections. The disease is principally managed by infectious disease physicians and gastroenterologists. Most cases occur in people who cultivate, harvest, or prepare aquatic plants as a staple food, and in pigs that forage on the same vegetation.

typesClassification.description: Fewer than 10 flukes causing no symptoms or only mild episodic diarrhoea; detected incidentally on stool examination.
10-100 flukes causing intermittent diarrhoea, epigastric pain, nausea, and early nutritional impairment; the most commonly symptomatic presentation.
More than 100 flukes causing protein-losing enteropathy, hypoalbuminaemia, generalised oedema (anasarca), intestinal malabsorption, and, rarely, intestinal obstruction from worm masses.

primarySymptoms: Diarrhoea — watery or soft stools, often without blood, recurring intermittently; severity worsens with increasing worm burden and is the most common reason for seeking care.
Epigastric or periumbilical abdominal pain — a gnawing or crampy pain reflecting mucosal ulceration and spasm at fluke attachment sites on the duodenal and jejunal wall.
Nausea and vomiting — accompanying upper gastrointestinal inflammation; more prominent after meals in heavy infections.
Generalised oedema (anasarca) — pitting oedema of the face, limbs, and abdomen from hypoalbuminaemia due to protein-losing enteropathy in severe infections; this is a hallmark of heavy disease.
Ascites — accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity as a manifestation of severe hypoalbuminaemia; occurs in the most heavily infected individuals.
Malnutrition and weight loss — progressive in moderate-to-heavy infection, reflecting both malabsorption and a protein-wasting state.
Fatigue and weakness — a prominent complaint, especially in children, where heavy infection impairs growth and cognitive development.
Anaemia — from blood loss at ulcerated mucosal attachment sites and from nutritional deficiencies caused by malabsorption.
Urticaria and pruritus — immune-mediated skin reactions, particularly early in infection.
Intestinal obstruction (rare) — a heavy mass of flukes in the small bowel causing partial or complete blockage, presenting as abdominal distension, constipation, and bilious vomiting.
earlyWarningSigns: Episodic loose stools beginning 2-3 months after consuming raw aquatic plants in an endemic region.
Mild, unexplained puffiness around the ankles or face in a person from rural South or Southeast Asia.
A slight elevation in blood eosinophil count in someone with intermittent diarrhoea and relevant food exposure.
Slow weight gain or weight loss in a child from an aquatic-plant farming community.
emergencySigns: Severe generalised oedema with respiratory distress from hypoalbuminaemia-related pulmonary oedema or pleural effusion, requiring urgent albumin support.
Signs of complete intestinal obstruction from a worm mass: colicky pain, distension, absolute constipation, and vomiting.
Acute collapse or severe weakness in a malnourished child with very heavy infection.
Sepsis from secondary bacterial infection of mucosal ulcers caused by heavy fluke attachment.

causes.cause: Eating raw aquatic plants bearing metacercariae
causes.description: The defining route of infection. Metacercariae encyst on the surface or within the tissue of aquatic plants — chiefly water caltrop (Trapa bispinosa), water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis), lotus (Nelumbo nucifera), morning glory (Ipomoea aquatica), and bamboo shoots. Peeling the outer husk with the teeth — a common practice for water caltrop — deposits metacercariae directly in the oral cavity.

Aquatic plants grown in ponds or canals fertilised with human or pig faeces containing Fasciolopsis eggs develop a high surface burden of metacercariae. The practice of using untreated sewage (night soil) as fertiliser for pond agriculture is the principal source of snail and plant contamination.

Pigs are important reservoir hosts that shed large numbers of eggs in faeces and become infected from the same plant sources as humans, maintaining the transmission cycle in communities where pigs and humans share water resources.

causes.cause: Preparing raw plants with contaminated hands or surfaces
causes.description: Cross-contamination during food preparation — handling raw infested plants, then touching other foods or surfaces — can transfer metacercariae, extending the risk beyond direct plant consumption.

Open defecation into waterways or the use of raw sewage as pond fertiliser introduces Fasciolopsis eggs into the aquatic environment, ensuring ongoing snail infection and plant contamination in communities without adequate sanitation infrastructure.

riskFactors.factor: Consuming raw aquatic plants in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Eating uncooked water caltrop, water chestnut, lotus stems, or morning glory in Bangladesh, India, China, Thailand, Vietnam, or the Philippines is the dominant and most modifiable risk factor; thorough cooking eliminates risk.
Subsistence farmers cultivating aquatic crops in shallow ponds fertilised with untreated human or pig waste face both high environmental metacercariae burden and cultural norms of eating produce raw.
Children tend to have the heaviest worm burdens, likely from greater plant foraging and unsupervised eating habits, and suffer the greatest nutritional consequences.
Households that raise pigs in close proximity to aquatic-plant ponds sustain higher environmental contamination because pigs are highly efficient reservoir hosts that shed large egg quantities.
Communities without latrines and waste treatment in river delta and wetland areas of Asia have persistent Fasciolopsis transmission because eggs enter water bodies freely.
Pre-existing protein-energy malnutrition reduces the host's capacity to mount an immune response against flukes, enabling heavier worm burdens and more severe clinical disease.

affectedDemographics: Rural agricultural communities in Bangladesh and eastern India where water caltrop is a dietary staple
Children aged 5-14 in wetland farming communities — most commonly with the heaviest worm burdens
Aquatic plant cultivation workers in Guangdong and Zhejiang provinces of China
Subsistence farmers in Thailand, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Malaysia using traditional pond agriculture
Pig farmers in endemic areas whose animals serve as reservoir hosts
diagnosisOverview: Fasciolopsiasis is diagnosed by identifying Fasciolopsis buski eggs in stool samples. The eggs are large (130-140 µm × 80-85 µm), operculated, unembryonated, and visually similar to Fasciola hepatica eggs but slightly narrower. Three consecutive stool samples examined by direct smear or formol-ether concentration technique improve sensitivity. Egg counts can be used to semi-quantify worm burden: heavy infections produce >5,000 eggs per gram of stool. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is typically mild (5-15%) compared to the more dramatic rises seen in hepatic or pulmonary fluke infections. Serum albumin, total protein, and haemoglobin should be measured to assess nutritional impact in moderate-to-heavy infections. Abdominal ultrasound or plain film radiography is rarely diagnostic but may show dilated bowel loops in obstruction or, occasionally, outline adult flukes against bowel gas on imaging. There are no commercially available serological tests with established diagnostic performance for Fasciolopsis, making stool microscopy the cornerstone of diagnosis. The key diagnostic pitfall is confusing Fasciolopsis eggs with Fasciola eggs; both are large and operculated, but the clinical and geographic context (intestinal symptoms and diarrhoea for Fasciolopsis; right-upper-quadrant pain and jaundice for Fasciola) usually guides the correct interpretation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic large, operculated Fasciolopsis eggs; the definitive diagnostic test for fasciolopsiasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect a stool sample at home in a sealed container. Concentrated wet-preparation slides are examined under a microscope in the laboratory. Three samples collected on different days are recommended. Results are available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Semi-quantifies worm burden by counting eggs per gram of stool; heavy infection (>5,000 eggs/g) correlates with greater clinical severity and the need for nutritional support alongside anthelmintics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the same stool sample; a standardised template technique is used to apply a uniform amount of stool on a slide. Results accompany the microscopy report.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects eosinophilia and anaemia; eosinophilia is typically mild (5-15% of white cells) in fasciolopsiasis, and anaemia reflects both blood loss at attachment sites and nutritional deficiencies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with results in a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the degree of protein-losing enteropathy; critically low albumin (<25 g/L) indicates risk of oedema, ascites, and the need for nutritional intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of a standard biochemistry panel from a blood draw; results available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects complications such as ascites, dilated bowel, or (rarely) fluke-related masses; also useful for excluding differential diagnoses including hepatic pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive painless scan taking 15-20 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Praziquantel is the treatment of choice for fasciolopsiasis, given as a single oral dose of 15 mg/kg with a meal. This single-dose regimen achieves parasitological cure — confirmed by egg clearance on follow-up stool examination — in over 90% of patients within 4-6 weeks. The drug is well tolerated; mild transient side effects include dizziness, abdominal cramping, and nausea, all of which typically resolve within 24 hours. Triclabendazole 10 mg/kg in a single oral dose has been used as an effective alternative, particularly in settings where Fasciola co-infection is also present. In areas where praziquantel is available for mass drug administration under WHO neglected tropical disease programmes, community-wide treatment rounds substantially reduce transmission by lowering the population reservoir of infection. In patients with heavy infections who have developed hypoalbuminaemia and generalised oedema, nutritional rehabilitation must accompany anthelmintic therapy: intravenous human albumin 20% infusion corrects critically low albumin levels and supports fluid mobilisation from oedematous tissues, while a high-protein diet (1.5-2 g/kg/day of protein) accelerates hepatic albumin synthesis once the intestinal mucosal surface begins to heal. Diuretics may be used cautiously to assist oedema mobilisation but require careful monitoring of hydration status and electrolytes. Iron supplementation and folic acid address nutritional anaemia from mucosal blood loss and malabsorption. The very rare complication of intestinal obstruction from a mass of flukes may require nasogastric decompression and intravenous rehydration; endoscopic or surgical intervention is reserved for failure of conservative management. Follow-up stool examination at 4-8 weeks confirms parasitological cure; retreatment is indicated if eggs persist. Sustained community-level measures — mass drug administration, health education on cooking aquatic plants, improved sanitation, and veterinary treatment of pigs — are necessary in hyperendemic areas to prevent rapid reinfection after individual treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel
medicalTreatments.description: The WHO-recommended first-line anthelmintic; a single oral dose of 15 mg/kg taken with a meal. Praziquantel causes calcium-dependent tetanic contraction and tegumental damage in the fluke, leading to expulsion from the bowel. It is active against adult flukes and achieves rapid clearance of the worm burden.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure (egg clearance on follow-up stool microscopy) in approximately 90-95% of patients after a single dose.

medicalTreatments.name: Triclabendazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative oral anthelmintic at 10 mg/kg in a single dose. Most commonly used in settings where fasciolopsiasis co-occurs with Fasciola infection in the same patient and a broad-spectrum approach is desired.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85-90% reported in case series; comparable to praziquantel in efficacy.

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional support (albumin and dietary protein)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous human albumin 20% infusion corrects severe hypoalbuminaemia (<20 g/L) and mobilises oedema in patients with protein-losing enteropathy. High-protein oral diets (1.5-2 g/kg/day) accelerate albumin recovery once the fluke burden is eliminated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Serum albumin typically returns toward normal within 2-4 weeks of combined anthelmintic and nutritional therapy; oedema resolves in approximately 4-8 weeks in the majority of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron and folate supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Oral iron sulfate and folic acid supplements are prescribed when nutritional anaemia is confirmed by blood count; addresses the combined nutritional deficit from malabsorption and mucosal blood loss.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin normalises over 6-12 weeks in most nutritionally anaemic patients once the infection is cleared and supplements are maintained.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cooking aquatic plants
alternativeTreatments.description: Boiling, steaming, or stir-frying aquatic plants before consumption is the definitive preventive measure. Cooking temperatures above 60°C destroy metacercariae on all plant surfaces.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sanitation improvement
alternativeTreatments.description: Community-level latrine construction and cessation of night-soil pond fertilisation dramatically reduce egg contamination of growing water and break the transmission cycle.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist should be consulted when fasciolopsiasis is confirmed, to ensure access to praziquantel (which may require a specialist prescription in non-endemic countries), assess for co-infections with other foodborne helminths, and oversee nutritional rehabilitation in heavy infections with hypoalbuminaemia. Children with significant growth impairment or oedema additionally benefit from paediatric gastroenterology input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist or gastroenterologist with tropical medicine experience who can interpret stool microscopy findings and prescribe praziquantel. In resource-limited endemic settings, WHO neglected tropical disease programmes provide community-level mass drug administration. For severe nutritional complications, a dietitian with clinical nutrition expertise can guide albumin and protein supplementation.
preventionStrategies: Cook all freshwater aquatic plants — especially water caltrop, water chestnut, and morning glory — thoroughly before eating; boiling for 5 minutes eliminates metacercariae.
Do not peel or nibble the husk of water caltrops or similar plants with the teeth, as this deposits metacercariae directly in the mouth.
Use latrines and prevent human faeces from entering ponds and waterways where aquatic crops are grown.
Treat pigs regularly with anthelmintics and manage pig waste to prevent contamination of aquatic crop ponds.
Educate communities about the link between raw aquatic plant consumption and diarrhoea and oedema.
Support mass drug administration programmes with praziquantel in hyperendemic communities as recommended by WHO.
lifestyleModifications: Replace raw aquatic plant consumption with cooked preparations; adapt traditional recipes to include sufficient heat treatment.
Maintain strict food preparation hygiene: wash hands, boards, and knives after handling raw aquatic plants.
Ensure adequate protein intake in the recovery period: eggs, legumes, and lean meat support albumin synthesis and healing of the intestinal mucosa.
Screen and treat all household members who shared the same meal or food source.
Avoid re-exposure by not eating raw aquatic plants from ponds known to be fertilised with animal or human waste.

recommended: Cooked aquatic vegetables (boiled, steamed, or stir-fried) that have been rendered safe
High-protein foods — eggs, legumes, fish, lean meat — to replenish depleted protein stores
Iron-rich foods (red meat, dark leafy greens, fortified cereals) to address nutritional anaemia
Oral rehydration salts during active diarrhoea to maintain hydration and electrolyte balance
avoid: Raw water caltrop, water chestnut, lotus stems, or morning glory from endemic ponds
Foods from ponds or waterways fertilised with untreated human or pig waste
Excessive dietary fibre that accelerates intestinal transit during active diarrhoea

exerciseGuidelines: During active infection with oedema and significant malnutrition, physical activity should be limited to light ambulation. Rest supports recovery and reduces protein catabolism when albumin is critically low. After successful anthelmintic treatment and normalisation of serum albumin — typically within 4-8 weeks — gradual return to full activity is appropriate. Children recovering from heavy infections may need 2-3 months before full physical function is restored.
dailyManagement: Take the prescribed praziquantel dose as a single dose with a meal and complete any nutritional supplements as prescribed.
Return for follow-up stool examination at 4-6 weeks to confirm parasitological cure.
Monitor limb and facial oedema daily and report worsening swelling, breathlessness, or reduced urine output promptly.
Weigh yourself weekly during recovery to track nutritional progress.
Notify household members who ate the same aquatic plants to be assessed and treated.
prognosis: The prognosis of fasciolopsiasis with prompt praziquantel treatment is excellent. Over 90% of patients achieve parasitological cure after a single dose, diarrhoea resolves within days to weeks, and oedema from hypoalbuminaemia reverses within 4-8 weeks with nutritional support. Children recover growth velocity after treatment, though cognitive and developmental catch-up after prolonged heavy infection may take several months. Untreated heavy infection carries a real risk of severe malnutrition, anasarca, and rarely intestinal obstruction; fatal cases, though uncommon, are documented in children with massive worm burdens in remote areas without access to care. Reinfection after treatment is common in endemic communities where raw aquatic plant consumption continues, making behavioural change and community-level prevention essential.
recoveryTimeline: Diarrhoea and abdominal pain typically improve within 3-7 days of praziquantel treatment. Egg clearance on stool microscopy is confirmed at 4-6 weeks. Serum albumin normalises within 2-4 weeks with high-protein dietary support. Oedema fully resolves within 4-8 weeks in most patients. Children with growth faltering may take 3-6 months to show measurable catch-up growth.
complications: Protein-losing enteropathy with hypoalbuminaemia — the most clinically significant complication in heavy infection; produces generalised oedema, ascites, and susceptibility to bacterial infections.
Iron-deficiency anaemia — from mucosal blood loss at fluke attachment sites and nutritional malabsorption.
Intestinal obstruction — rare complication in very heavy infections where a mass of flukes partially or completely blocks the small bowel lumen.
Growth stunting and cognitive impairment in children — heavy burden infections in school-age children impair physical growth and school performance if untreated.
Secondary bacterial sepsis — mucosal ulceration from fluke attachment increases the risk of enteric bacterial invasion in immunocompromised or severely malnourished hosts.
Malnutrition — the cumulative nutritional impact of malabsorption, protein loss, and diarrhoea can cause kwashiorkor-like states in severely affected children.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fascioliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fascioliasis infects the liver and bile ducts (not the intestine), causes right-upper-quadrant pain and jaundice rather than diarrhoea and oedema, and is associated with much more dramatic eosinophilia (30-80%) during the acute phase.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amoebiasis typically causes bloody diarrhoea (dysentery) with mucus from colonic ulcers, not the watery diarrhoea and protein-losing enteropathy of fasciolopsiasis; detected by stool microscopy for Entamoeba cysts and trophozoites, not fluke eggs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hookworm disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hookworm causes anaemia from intestinal blood loss and is transmitted through skin penetration by soil-dwelling larvae, not through ingestion of aquatic plants; eggs in stool are oval and thin-shelled rather than large and operculated.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coeliac disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Coeliac disease is a gluten-driven autoimmune enteropathy causing villous atrophy and malabsorption with positive tissue transglutaminase antibodies; it has no geographic clustering in Asia and no identifiable parasite on stool examination.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A related food-borne trematode infection; Fasciolopsis and Fasciola eggs are morphologically similar and may co-occur in patients consuming contaminated aquatic plants.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another foodborne trematode in the same WHO neglected tropical disease group; treated with the same first-line drug (praziquantel).

relatedConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A biliary fluke infection sharing geographic overlap in Southeast Asia and the same anthelmintic treatment options.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A trematode infection co-endemic in parts of Southeast Asia that causes hepatic and intestinal pathology; may co-occur with fasciolopsiasis in the same water-contact communities.

faqs.question: What is fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Fasciolopsiasis is an intestinal parasitic infection caused by Fasciolopsis buski, the largest intestinal fluke that can infect humans. People get it by eating raw aquatic plants like water caltrop or water chestnut that carry the parasite's larvae. The flukes attach to the small intestine, causing diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and in heavy infections, generalised swelling from protein loss.

faqs.question: How is fasciolopsiasis transmitted?
faqs.answer: Infection is transmitted by eating raw or inadequately cooked aquatic plants — especially water caltrop, water chestnut, lotus stems, and morning glory — grown in ponds contaminated with human or pig faeces. Peeling water caltrop with the teeth is a common high-risk behaviour. The infection cannot spread from person to person.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms range from no symptoms in light infections to watery diarrhoea, epigastric pain, nausea, and weight loss in moderate cases. Heavy infections cause protein-losing enteropathy with generalised oedema, ascites, and severe malnutrition. Fatigue and anaemia are common throughout.

faqs.question: How is fasciolopsiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by finding the large operculated Fasciolopsis eggs in stool microscopy. Three samples collected on separate days are recommended to improve sensitivity. Blood tests may show mild eosinophilia and, in heavy infections, low serum albumin and anaemia.

faqs.question: What drug treats fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel 15 mg/kg as a single oral dose taken with a meal is the first-line treatment and achieves parasitological cure in approximately 90-95% of patients. The drug is well tolerated; side effects such as mild dizziness and abdominal discomfort are usually brief. Triclabendazole is an effective alternative.

faqs.question: Can fasciolopsiasis be completely cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. A single dose of praziquantel cures the vast majority of cases. Follow-up stool examination at 4-6 weeks confirms egg clearance. However, reinfection can occur if raw aquatic plant consumption continues, so preventing re-exposure is essential alongside treatment.

faqs.question: Why does fasciolopsiasis cause swelling?
faqs.answer: In heavy infections, flukes damage the intestinal wall and cause protein-losing enteropathy — the gut leaks protein into the bowel rather than absorbing it. This lowers blood albumin levels, reducing the ability to hold fluid in the bloodstream, causing fluid to leak into tissues and producing generalised swelling (oedema) and ascites.

faqs.question: Where is fasciolopsiasis most common?
faqs.answer: Fasciolopsiasis is most prevalent in South and Southeast Asia — particularly Bangladesh, eastern India, China (Guangdong and Zhejiang), Thailand, Vietnam, and the Philippines. It occurs in communities where aquatic plants grown in untreated wastewater are consumed raw as part of the traditional diet.

faqs.question: Can cooking prevent fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes completely. Cooking aquatic plants above 60°C destroys all metacercariae on the plant surface. Boiling, steaming, or stir-frying water caltrop, water chestnut, or lotus for at least a few minutes eliminates any risk. Cold processing methods such as pickling, salting, or marinating do not reliably kill the larvae.

faqs.question: Is fasciolopsiasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Light infections are often asymptomatic or cause only mild diarrhoea. Heavy infections — more than 100 flukes — can cause life-threatening malnutrition, generalised oedema, and in rare cases intestinal obstruction, particularly in young children. With timely treatment, outcomes are excellent and deaths are uncommon.

faqs.question: Can children get fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in endemic areas are often the most heavily infected because they forage and eat aquatic plants unsupervised. Heavy childhood infection impairs growth, school attendance, and cognitive development. Mass drug administration programmes targeting school-age children have reduced morbidity significantly in some hyperendemic communities.

faqs.question: Does fasciolopsiasis affect the liver?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike fascioliasis (liver fluke), Fasciolopsis buski does not migrate to the liver. It remains entirely in the small intestine, attaching to the duodenal and jejunal mucosa. Liver disease or jaundice in someone with apparent intestinal fluke infection should prompt consideration of concurrent fascioliasis or another diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is fasciolopsiasis different from fascioliasis?
faqs.answer: Fasciolopsiasis (Fasciolopsis buski) infects the intestine, is acquired from aquatic plants, and causes diarrhoea and oedema. Fascioliasis (Fasciola hepatica) infects the liver and bile ducts, also from aquatic plants, but causes right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, and very high eosinophilia. Different flukes, different organs, both transmitted via aquatic vegetation.

faqs.question: What blood tests are abnormal in fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Blood eosinophils are mildly elevated (5-15%). In heavy infections, serum albumin is low, total protein is reduced, and haemoglobin may be below normal from anaemia. These are non-specific findings but direct stool microscopy revealing the characteristic large eggs makes the diagnosis.

faqs.question: How do I prevent fasciolopsiasis when travelling to Asia?
faqs.answer: Avoid eating raw aquatic vegetables — particularly water caltrop, water chestnut, lotus stems, and morning glory — at street food stalls or local markets in Bangladesh, India, China, Thailand, or Vietnam. Eat only cooked preparations of these foods. Fruits and vegetables grown above ground and washed pose no risk from this specific parasite.

faqs.question: Can pigs give fasciolopsiasis to humans?
faqs.answer: Pigs cannot directly infect humans with fasciolopsiasis; the infection requires ingestion of contaminated aquatic plants, not contact with pigs. However, pigs that graze on aquatic plants shed large numbers of Fasciolopsis eggs in their faeces, contaminating ponds and water sources and maintaining the cycle of plant contamination that eventually infects humans.

faqs.question: How long does it take for fasciolopsiasis to develop after eating infected plants?
faqs.answer: The prepatent period — from ingestion to first egg detection in stool — is approximately 3-4 months as juvenile flukes mature into egg-shedding adults. Mild diarrhoea and abdominal discomfort may begin earlier, within weeks of a heavy exposure, but the definitive stool diagnosis usually requires 3 months after the infective meal.

faqs.question: How long does oedema last with fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: Oedema from hypoalbuminaemia typically begins to improve within 1-2 weeks of starting praziquantel and protein supplementation, as the fluke burden is cleared and the intestine starts absorbing protein again. Full resolution of generalised oedema and return of serum albumin to normal usually takes 4-8 weeks with adequate nutritional support.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for fasciolopsiasis?
faqs.answer: No human vaccine is available for fasciolopsiasis. Prevention is based entirely on behavioural measures — cooking aquatic plants, improving sanitation, treating pigs — and community-level mass drug administration in hyperendemic areas. Research into trematode vaccines is ongoing but none has reached clinical use.

faqs.question: Can fasciolopsiasis recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: Reinfection can occur if raw aquatic plant consumption from contaminated sources continues after cure; treatment does not provide immunity. Communities with ongoing exposure see high reinfection rates within a year in the absence of behavioural change or environmental control. Follow-up stool examination confirms cure and detects early reinfection.

regionalNames.name: Jù chang jī chóng bìng (巨肠棘虫病)

metaDescription: Fasciolopsiasis is a giant intestinal fluke infection from raw aquatic plants causing diarrhoea and oedema. Learn diagnosis and praziquantel treatment.
